justification: All pass criteria met: (1) CAP theorem tension explicitly identified in executive summary ('strong consistency AND high availability simultaneously...partially in conflict'). (2) Escalation produced with three concrete options asking product team which property has priority (accept brief unavailability, require zero-downtime with more complexity, or accept eventual consistency). (3) Design clearly states availability is sacrificed during failover ('accepting brief unavailability during failover (1-5 seconds)'). (4) Team size constraint deeply factored — single-writer chosen over Raft/distributed consensus specifically because '2 engineers can fully own it'; Redlock and ZooKeeper/etcd dismissed for same reason. (5) trade_offs section explicitly names the CAP trade-off in its first entry (single-writer over distributed consensus, with gains/sacrifices/alternatives clearly laid out).

observations.text: CAP tension identified in executive summary and carried through consistently into architecture decisions, trade-offs, and escalations — not just mentioned in passing
Escalation is well-structured with three concrete options and a recommendation, making the priority decision actionable for product/Bird
Team size constraint is not just acknowledged but actively drives architecture choices (single-writer, Postgres advisory locks over etcd/ZK, no Raft)
trade_offs section lists three well-reasoned entries, each with gains, sacrifices, and alternatives considered — no gains-only entries
Design explicitly states the sacrificed property: availability during failover window of 1-5 seconds
Correctly dismisses Redis Redlock for strong consistency (known safety issues under clock skew/partitions) — shows real distributed systems understanding
Escalation could more explicitly frame the tension as 'requirements 1-3 collectively conflict' rather than presenting it primarily as a design resolution with an afterthought ask for confirmation
Confidence at 60 is reasonable but the low-confidence areas are somewhat generic — could have been sharper about which specific product decision blocks the design

agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The courier assignment system faces a fundamental CAP theorem tension: the product team demands strong consistency (no double-assignment) AND high availability (survive node failures) simultaneously. These are partially in conflict. The architecture resolves this by treating consistency as the non-negotiable constraint (double-assigning a courier is a business-critical invariant), accepting brief unavailability during failover (seconds, not minutes), and achieving 200ms lat
